Proof: Suppose the operation can be performed in \(o(\log n)\). Given \(n\) unsorted numbers, sort them as follows:
a. Build a valid 2--3 tree whose leaves all contain the value 0. As we have seen, this can be done in \(\mathcal{O}(n)\).
b. Allocate an additional array \(B\) that holds \(n\) pointers to the leaves of the tree. This array can also be built in \(\mathcal{O}(n)\) during the tree construction.

d. Extract from the tree a linked list containing its leaves in \(\mathcal{O}(n)\).

Altogether we obtain a sort in \(o(n \log n)\), contradicting the comparison-based lower bound.

Algorithm description and running-time analysis:
We build an array containing all endpoints of all segments, marking for each point whether it is a start point or an end point. First, we sort the array so that the points are ordered from left to right. In case of a tie (equal points), start points are placed before end points. Second, we scan the array and, at each step, maintain the number of start points seen minus the number of end points seen, simply by adding 1 at a start point and subtracting 1 at an end point. It is easy to see that this counter equals exactly the number of segments that contain the current point; it is also easy to see that if there are three segments that share a common point, then there is such a point that is, in particular, an endpoint of a segment. Therefore, we return ``yes'' if and only if the counter reaches at some step a value of at least \(3\). The sorting takes \(O(n \log n)\) time and the scan is linear, so the total running time is \(O(n \log n)\).

\textbf{Algorithm:}
Maintain an auxiliary heap. In the auxiliary heap we keep some of the keys of the original heap, with pointers to the corresponding nodes in the original heap. Initially we copy the root of the original heap into the auxiliary heap.
At each step we perform \textsc{ExtractMax} on the auxiliary heap. We print the extracted element $x$. We then insert its two children (in the original heap) into the auxiliary heap. Repeat this until $k$ elements have been printed.

\textbf{Correctness:}
If the key of a node is not among the $k$ largest keys, then it should not be printed, and necessarily neither should its children. Note that at every step the auxiliary heap contains all the potential elements to be printed, namely all the children (from the original heap) of the nodes that have already been printed. Any node whose parent is printed is inserted into the auxiliary heap, and any node whose parent is not printed is not inserted. Therefore the algorithm prints the $k$ largest values, in fact in decreasing order.

\textbf{Running time analysis:}
At each step we remove one element from the auxiliary heap and insert two in its place. Hence the size of the auxiliary heap is at most $k+1$. Therefore a basic heap operation takes time $O(\log k)$.
Finding the two children takes $O(1)$ time (because we have a pointer to the corresponding node in the original heap). Altogether this is $O(\log k)$ per step.
At each step we perform three operations on the auxiliary heap (one \textsc{Extract} and two \textsc{Insert}s). There are $k$ such steps. Altogether this is $O(k\log k)$.
Therefore the total running time is $O(k\log k)$.
Note that in the last $k/2$ steps the heap already has size at least $k/2$, so a basic heap operation takes $\Omega(\log k)$ time; hence the total running time is also $\Omega(k\log k)$, and therefore $\Theta(k\log k)$.

Amortized analysis of the operation in the structure:
Consider a sequence of $N$ operations. In such a sequence, at most $N$ elements have been inserted into the structure (by Enqueue and PEnqueue).
If an element was inserted by PEnqueue (i.e., into stack $B$), then in total at most two operations are performed on it: a Push to $B$ and possibly a Pop from $B$.
If an element was inserted by Enqueue (i.e., into stack $A$), then in total at most four operations are performed on it: a Push to $A$, possibly a Pop from $A$, a Push to $B$, and possibly a Pop from $B$.
Thus, in any case we spend $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time per element.
Therefore, over any sequence of $N$ operations the running time is $\mathcal{O}(N)$. It is also clear that the running time is $\Omega(N)$, since every operation in the sequence takes $\Omega(1)$.
Hence any sequence, and in particular the worst one, takes $\Theta(N)$ time.
The amortized time per operation is therefore $\Theta(1)$.

It is easy to see that if no deletes are performed, then the total cost is $O(m + k \log n)$. We perform an amortized analysis and prove that even if deletes are performed, the cost remains the same. We use the bank method. Each \texttt{delete} deposits two coins. An expensive \texttt{delete}, one that rebuilds the tree, withdraws a number of coins equal to the size of the tree. Suppose we performed an expensive \texttt{delete} on a tree of size $x$, in which $x/2$ of the nodes are marked as deleted. Then the operations that caused them to be marked as deleted deposited $2 \cdot x/2 = x$ coins, and therefore we will never be in overdraft.

In total, we obtain that the total cost is $O(m + k \log n)$.

Proof: We show a lower bound of $\Omega(n\log n)$. Let $T(n)$ denote the worst-case (W.C.) running time of the best algorithm for the above problem under the comparison model. We show how to build from such an algorithm a sorting algorithm: take an array of size $n$. Build from it a heap in worst-case $O(n)$ time. Add the pointers $s$ in time $T(n)$. Find the minimum in $O(1)$ time. Scan the heap using the pointers: start by printing the minimum, and at each step move to the next element via the pointer $s$ and print it. We obtain a sorting algorithm that runs in time $T(n)+O(n)$. All the operations performed on the original keys are comparisons (also inside the BuildHeap algorithm). Therefore the algorithm fits the comparison model. By the lower bound shown in class, any such algorithm requires worst-case time $\Omega(n\log n)$. Therefore necessarily $T(n)\in\Omega(n\log n)$.

Common mistakes:
- Proving an upper bound instead of a lower bound (i.e., proposing a specific algorithm that solves the problem within the claimed time).
- Ignoring the time to build the heap (the proof in class started from an array of $n$ elements, not from a heap that already satisfies some invariant).
- Proving only that updating each specific element cannot take $o(\log n)$ (i.e., there exists an element for which it takes longer). This does not imply that one cannot update all elements in time $o(n\log n)$.

The number of random numbers drawn is always on the order of $n$, and therefore the expectation is also $n$. The reason: (assume all elements are distinct) at each step when a pivot is chosen, one element is removed from the set of elements that continue to the next stage. Therefore, until we reach a constant input size, the number of pivots that are drawn is linear in $n$.

The worst case is unbounded, since it is possible to draw infinitely many times with the partition never satisfying the condition. The probability of success (a partition satisfying the condition) is \(\frac{3}{5}\) (by part (b)). This is a geometric random variable with expectation \(\frac{5}{3}\), i.e., in expectation a good partition is found after \(\frac{5}{3}\) draws. The number of comparisons in each draw is \(n\), hence the expected number of comparisons is \(\frac{5n}{3}\).

Many answers claimed ``it is like quicksort,'' which is not correct. In quicksort, the number of comparisons per level is always $n$, and the expected number of levels is $O(\log n)$. In contrast, here the expected number of comparisons per level is linear in $n$, whereas the number of levels is always $O(\log n)$ because the partition guarantees that the smaller part has size at least $n/5$, which bounds the number of levels by $\log_{5/4} n$. Another way to prove this is to write a recurrence for the expected number of comparisons and solve it using the Master Theorem (you need to show which case applies) or a recursion tree.

Note: If every search in the table, including an unsuccessful search, probes at most $k$ cells, then searching for an element that is present in the table probes at most $k-1$ cells. The elements of $X_1$ remain in the same places they had in the first table; hence searching for an element from $X_1$ probes at most $k_1-1$ cells. Elements from $X_2$ can fill all the ``holes'' between the elements of $X_1$, therefore:
- search for an element from $X_2$: $n_1+n_2$
- search for an element not in the table: $n_1+n_2+1$

The number of elements in each list is bounded by the number of cells that must be read from the table (minus one, because an unsuccessful search would require reading an empty cell); therefore:
A search for an element from $X_1$ will require reading at most $k_1-1$ keys.
A search for an element from $X_2$ will require reading at most $(k_1-1)+(k_2-1)$, that is, $k_1+k_2-2$ keys (if the lists are chained to the same cell).
Also, a search for an element that is neither in $X_1$ nor in $X_2$ will require reading at most $k_1+k_2-2$ keys, since this is the maximal list length.

According to the deletion algorithm, after the deletion the table is in the same state as if the element had never been inserted; therefore, the table state is identical to that in which only the elements of \(X_2\) were inserted. Implementation of DeleteBetween:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Perform a split: $T_{<a},\, T_{\ge a} \leftarrow \mathrm{Split}(T, a)$, where $T_{<a}$ is a red-black tree whose elements are less than $a$, and $T_{\ge a}$ is a red-black tree whose elements are equal to $a$ or greater.
\item Now perform another split: $T_{[a,b]},\, T_{\ge b} \leftarrow \mathrm{Split}(T_{\ge a}, b)$, where $T_{[a,b]}$ is a red-black tree whose elements are less than $b$ and greater than or equal to $a$, and $T_{\ge b}$ is a red-black tree whose elements are greater than or equal to $b$.
\item Finally, join: $T \leftarrow \mathrm{Join}(T_{<a}, T_{\ge b})$, and return the tree that contains all elements except those between $a$ and $b$.
\end{enumerate}

This uses a constant number of red-black tree operations, each taking time $O(\log n)$.

Note that there is no need to store all \(n\) bids, but only the highest bid for each product. We keep in a Fibonacci max-heap \(H\) the highest bid for product \(j\) (key = price \(p\), value = product index \(j\)). In addition, we maintain an array of \(m\) pointers into \(H\) (at position \(j\), a pointer to the bid for product \(j\)).

Init: Build an empty heap \(H\), insert into it a bid with price zero for each of the products, and store the pointers in the array.

Bid: If \(p\) is greater than the current bid for product \(j\) (which we can find in constant time via the pointer), then perform Increase-Key in \(H\) for this product; otherwise ignore (we assume the product has not yet been sold). In a Fibonacci heap this costs \(O(1)\) amortized.

Sell: Perform Delete-Max on \(H\) and return the product \(j\) and its price \(p\). There are at most \(m\) bids in \(H\), so this costs \(O(\log m)\) amortized.

Here we must keep all bids, because the same product type can be sold multiple times on different days. In addition to the heap \(H\) and the array of pointers as in part (a), we maintain an array of \(m\) max-heaps (lazy binomial or Fibonacci). \(H_j\) contains all bids pending for product type \(j\). The heap \(H\) contains the heads of all heaps \(H_j\), i.e., the best bid for each type.

Init: For every \(j\), build an empty heap \(H_j\) and insert a zero bid into it. Build \(H\) from the heads of the heaps and store pointers in an array.

Bid: Perform \textsc{Insert} of \(p\) into \(H_j\). If its maximum changed (in particular, if it increased), perform \textsc{Increase-Key} as in part (a) to update in \(H\) the best bid for product type \(j\). Overall \(O(1)\) amortized for the two operations.

Sell: Perform \textsc{Delete-Max} twice on \(H\) to identify the two product types \(j_1, j_2\) and the best bids \(p_1, p_2\), respectively. Perform \textsc{Delete-Max} also on the two heaps \(H_{j_1}\) and \(H_{j_2}\) to remove those bids, and insert into \(H\) the new heads of those heaps, which are the next bids for those product types. Update the pointer array.

Total running time: \(O(\log n) = O(\log m) + O(\log n)\) amortized, since by the given \(2m \ge n\), and \(H\) has at most \(m\) elements and each \(H_j\) has at most \(n\).

We show how to compute the maximum overlap between a suffix of $s$ and a prefix of $t$; perform the same procedure with the roles reversed and return the maximum of the two.

Build the suffix tree $T$ for $s$ in time $O(n)$. Now search $t$ in $T$ by starting at the root and walking along edges as long as there is a match between the characters of $t$ and the substring written on the edge (this is essentially searching for $t$ in the uncompressed trie).

At every visit to a node $x$ that has a leaf child $y$ and the edge $xy$ is labeled only with the symbol $\$$, update $k$ to be the number of characters of $t$ read so far (since we have found an overlap between a prefix of $t$ and a suffix of $s$).

If we reach the end of $t$ in the middle of an edge, and the only character left on the edge that we have not scanned is $\$$, then update $k$ to be $m$.

The scan takes $m$ steps, hence the total running time is $O(n+m)$.

Note: One can obtain a running time of $O(\min(m,n))$ if we work with the prefix of $t$ and the suffix of $s$ of length $\min(m,n)$.

The algorithm we describe is a variation on Quick Sort.
Choose a random nut \(x\), and partition the bolts with respect to \(x\). We have thus found the bolts larger than the nut, those smaller than it, and the bolt equal to it, \(y\). Partition the nuts with respect to \(y\). Now continue recursively on the set of bolts larger than \(x\) and nuts larger than \(y\), and on the set of bolts smaller than \(x\) and nuts smaller than \(y\).
The recursion terminates when \(n=1\).

The running time for the recursive calls is \(T(k-1) + T(n-k)\). Since \(x\) is chosen at random, the expected running time is \(E[T(n)] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big(E[T(k-1)] + E[T(n-k)]\big)\), and as we saw for Quick Sort the solution to this recurrence is \(E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)\).

We will prove that any algorithm for the problem runs in time $\Omega(n \log n)$.
There are $n!$ possible matchings between the bolts and the nuts. Each comparison has one of three outcomes, and therefore the algorithm’s decision tree is ternary. For the tree to have $n!$ distinct leaves, its height (both maximal and average) must be at least $\log_3(n!) = \Omega(n \log n)$.

Note: This cannot be established by appealing to the sorting lower bound discussed in class, since the problem does not require sorting the nuts and bolts by diameter. Likewise, an example with a sorted array of nuts and an unsorted array of bolts does not prove an $\Omega(n \log n)$ lower bound, because the matching can be performed so that the result is not sorted, and moreover this is not a general sorting instance—the presence of a sorted array adds substantial information about the input.

We store the page numbers in the cache as a queue (a linked list ordered by recency—the most recent at the back of the queue) with a length field, and we also keep a hash table that maps each page $x$ present in the cache to its list node in the queue.

If $x$ does not appear in the hash table, create a list node for it, insert it at the end of the queue, and store $x$ in the hash table together with the pointer to the node.
If the length of the queue has grown to $n+1$, then delete the page $y$ that is now at the head of the queue, both from the queue and from the hash table.

We used a constant number of operations in the hash table (each $O(1)$ in expectation) and in the queue (each $O(1)$, since we accessed the head, the tail, or via a pointer).

Find the median of the array and count how many times it occurs in \(\mathcal{O}(n)\). If it occurs at least \(n/k\) times, return it. Otherwise, proceed recursively on the two subarrays—those to the right and to the left of the median. At stage \(i\), the array size is \(\left\lfloor \frac{n}{2^i} \right\rfloor\), so we are guaranteed to stop when \(2^i = k\). The total running time is \(\mathcal{O}(n \log k)\): the running time of each stage is \(\mathcal{O}(n)\), and there are at most \(\log k\) stages.

Analogously to the calculation for WAVL:
Let $S_k$ denote the minimal number of nodes in an $i$-WAVL tree whose root has rank $k$. Then
\[ S_k = 1 + 2S_{k-i}, \]
a recurrence whose solution is
\[ S_k = 2^{\lfloor k/i \rfloor + 1} - 1. \]
Hence, if the tree has $n$ nodes, the rank of the root satisfies
\[ k \le i \log_2(n+1). \]
In an $i$-WAVL tree we have height $\le$ rank, therefore the above bound is also a bound on the height of the tree.

Algorithm description:
Build a suffix tree for the string of length \(n\). Traverse the tree as follows: from the root, search for the letters \texttt{ab}. If no path starts with \texttt{ab}, return \texttt{false}.
Afterward, continue traversing the tree and look for the letters \texttt{b} and \texttt{a}. If we find the letter \texttt{a}, check whether the remainder of its edge leads to an internal node; if so, return \texttt{true}. If not, or if we did not find \texttt{a}, continue along the path led by the letter \texttt{b}.

Running-time justification:
- Building the tree: \(O(n)\).
- Traversing a path in the tree that starts with \texttt{a} and then consists only of a sequence of \texttt{b}'s — a path of length at most \(O(n)\), with each comparison taking at most \(O(1)\); altogether \(O(n)\).
- At each step along the path, checking whether there is a branch labeled \texttt{a}, and whether it leads to an internal node — \(O(1)\cdot n = O(n)\).

Description of the data structures used for the implementation (and the purpose of each data structure):
We need to maintain 3 data structures: one to store the board, one to store the contents of the columns, and one to store the contents of the rows. When we write “matrix” we mean a two-dimensional array of size $n \times n$.
- For the board — we maintain a matrix $A$ such that the board’s content at position $i,j$ is $A[i,j]$, and $0$ indicates that the cell is empty.
- For the row contents — we maintain a matrix $B$ such that if $num$ appears in row $i$ then $B[i,num]=1$, and otherwise $0$.
- For the column contents — we maintain a matrix $C$ such that if $num$ appears in column $j$ then $C[j,num]=1$, and otherwise $0$.

Description of the operations:
- Contains$(i,j)$: if $A[i,j] \ne 0$, return $A[i,j]$.
- write$(i,j,num)$: if $B[i,num]=0$ and $C[j,num]=0$, assign $C[j,num]=1$, $B[i,num]=1$, and $A[i,j]=num$; otherwise report that the operation is illegal.
- Erase$(i,j)$: let $num = A[i,j]$ and assign $B[i,num]=0$, $C[j,num]=0$, and $A[i,j]=0$.

Time and space complexity:
Initialization is $O(n^2)$ due to allocating 3 two-dimensional arrays. Each operation runs in constant time, since there is a constant number of array accesses.

Description of the operations:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Contains\((i,j)\): if \(A[i,j] \neq 0\) return \(A[i,j]\).
